_comment: Per-process-family environmental coefficients, calibrated to published anchor data. ALL numbers are ILLUSTRATIVE estimates for a weekend educational tool — they are derived from published aggregate data and industry averages, not from foundry-specific measurements. See methodology in sources.json.

_units.waterL: liters of ultrapure water per process step per 300mm wafer
_units.energyKwh: kilowatt-hours of electricity per process step per 300mm wafer (includes tool power, amortized facility overhead)
_units.ghgScope2KgCo2e: kg CO2e per process step per wafer from electricity (at global industry-avg grid intensity ~0.42 kg CO2e/kWh, 2023)
_units.pfasKgCo2e: kg CO2e per process step per wafer from fluorinated GHG emissions after typical abatement (~95% DRE)

_calibration.anchor1: IMEC reports ~1,600 kg CO2eq per wafer for advanced N2 logic (imec-digest-2025). Etch + litho ≈ 40% of that. Scope 2 (electricity) ≈ 60% of total.
_calibration.anchor2: Hu 2023 industry avg: 8.22 L water / cm2 silicon. For a 300mm wafer (~700 cm2 usable), that's ~5,750 L per wafer across all process steps.
_calibration.anchor3: Bardon 2020 scaling: N28 to N2 shows 3.46x electricity, 2.3x water, 2.5x GHG per wafer.
_calibration.note: Per-step values below assume a representative step count for a ~7nm node flow (~1,200 process steps total, ~80 masks). Users of this data should treat values as order-of-magnitude correct for educational comparison, not as audit-grade.

processes.name: EUV Lithography
processes.description: Extreme Ultraviolet lithography at 13.5nm wavelength. Used for critical layers at N7 and below. ~5-20 EUV layers per advanced node.
processes.notes: EUV tools (ASML NXE/EXE series) draw ~1 MW each and have lower wafers-per-hour throughput than DUV, making per-wafer energy dramatically higher. Water use is mainly cooling and optics conditioning, not wafer immersion.

processes.name: DUV Lithography (193nm immersion)
processes.description: 193nm immersion lithography for non-critical layers. Used heavily in multi-patterning at older nodes.
processes.notes: Uses water as immersion fluid between lens and wafer. Throughput ~2-3x higher than EUV, so per-wafer energy is lower.

processes.name: Plasma Dry Etch
processes.description: Fluorine-chemistry plasma etching to pattern critical features. Uses CF4, C4F8, SF6, CHF3. Primary source of F-gas emissions.
processes.notes: Despite ~95% abatement (destruction/removal efficiency), etch remains the top GHG contributor due to high-GWP gases. SF6 has GWP100 of 25,200. CF4 has atmospheric lifetime of 50,000 years. PFAS regulation is placing this process family under increasing scrutiny.

processes.name: CVD Chamber Clean (NF3)
processes.description: NF3 remote plasma clean of CVD chamber walls between depositions. High utilization efficiency (~98%) but NF3 itself has GWP100 of 17,400.
processes.notes: The industry switched from C2F6 to NF3 for chamber cleaning because NF3 has higher utilization (~98% vs ~30%), dramatically reducing emitted GHGs despite NF3's own high GWP. SK Hynix reported optimizing 13 NF3 processes reduced emissions by ~12,000 tCO2e/year (2023).

processes.name: Wet Clean / Wet Bench
processes.description: Ultrapure water rinsing of wafers between major process steps. Dominant contributor to water consumption.
processes.notes: Wet cleans happen after nearly every major step, often 100-200 per wafer. A single advanced-node wafer can consume 5,000-7,000 liters of ultrapure water across its full process flow, dominated by these rinse steps.

processes.name: Physical Vapor Deposition (Sputtering)
processes.description: Sputtering of metals (Cu, Ti, TiN, Ta, TaN) for interconnect barriers and seed layers.
processes.notes: PVD generally uses minimal fluorinated gases; environmental cost is dominated by electricity and inert process gas (Ar) supply.

processes.name: Chemical Vapor Deposition / Atomic Layer Deposition
processes.description: Conformal thin-film deposition (SiO2, SiN, high-k dielectrics, W, TiN). ALD used for thinnest, most uniform films at advanced nodes.
processes.notes: ALD step counts explode at advanced nodes (gate-all-around structures, multi-patterning spacers). Each ALD step is small, but hundreds of them add up.

processes.name: Chemical Mechanical Planarization
processes.description: Polishing of wafer surface after metal deposition or oxide fill. Uses large volumes of slurry (abrasive + chemistry) and DI water for post-CMP cleans.
processes.notes: CMP water use is dominated by post-CMP rinses (the wafer arrives coated in slurry and must be rinsed to atomic cleanliness). Some modern CMP slurries contain PFAS-class chemistries under regulatory scrutiny.

processes.name: Ion Implantation
processes.description: Ion beam doping to introduce B, P, As into silicon. Vacuum-based, low water, moderate energy.
processes.notes: Implant is one of the least environmentally intensive front-end steps per operation. Uses specialty source gases but in tiny quantities.

processes.name: Inspection & Metrology
processes.description: Optical, e-beam, and scatterometry measurements for defect detection and critical-dimension control.
processes.notes: Minor per-step footprint, but step counts explode at advanced nodes (every litho and etch pair gets inspected). Worth showing for context.

processes.name: Thermal Processing (Anneal, Oxidation, Diffusion)
processes.description: High-temperature steps in furnaces or RTP tools for dopant activation, oxide growth, silicidation.
processes.notes: Thermal steps use significant electricity to maintain furnace and RTP temperatures. Lower step count than litho/etch/clean at advanced nodes but individually energy-heavy.
